title: Industrial Property Identification
name: Industrial Property Identification
metaTitle: Industrial Property Identification in San Antonio, TX
metaDescription: Industrial property identification for San Antonio 1031 exchanges: sourcing distribution, flex, and manufacturing replacement product along I-35 and I-10.

sections.html:
Industrial replacement property in San Antonio clusters along two corridors - I-35 running north toward Austin and south toward the border, and I-10 running east toward Houston and west through Eagle Ford-adjacent energy service territory - and identifying the right building means matching the exchange proceeds to a specific corridor's tenant demand rather than treating industrial space as one uniform category.

This describes the identification and sourcing process for industrial assets; it is not tax advice, and the underlying exchange strategy should be confirmed with a tax advisor.

Getting the corridor and building type right at identification matters more for industrial than for most other asset classes, since the specialized nature of the product means a mismatched building is harder to reposition after closing than a generic office or retail space would be.

sections.heading: Industrial Corridors Feeding San Antonio Replacement Demand
sections.html:
The I-35 corridor carries the heaviest distribution and logistics demand, driven in part by cross-border trade movement and growth spilling south from the Austin metro. The I-10 corridor west of downtown picks up service and equipment-yard demand tied to Eagle Ford energy activity, while I-10 east feeds regional distribution toward Houston. Each corridor supports a different tenant profile, lease structure, and building specification, which affects how an identified property should be underwritten before the 45-day window closes.

A JBSA-adjacent submarket also supports steady demand for smaller industrial and flex space serving contractors and suppliers connected to base operations, which is a distinct tenant pool from the larger cross-dock distribution buildings found along the core I-35 corridor.

sections.heading: Distinguishing Distribution, Flex, and Manufacturing Product for Identification
sections.html:
A distribution building built for cross-dock trailer access reads very differently in underwriting than a flex building split between office and warehouse space, or a manufacturing facility with heavy power service and specialized floor loading. Identification notices should describe the specific building and its use classification precisely, since a vague description of type of product identified rather than the actual parcel does not satisfy the unambiguous-description standard.

An investor targeting cold-storage or last-mile distribution product should confirm the building's specific mechanical and dock configuration matches that use before identification, since retrofitting a standard warehouse for those purposes can add cost and time that a 180-day exchange period does not accommodate.

sections.heading: Diligence Items Specific to Industrial Replacement Property
sections.html:
Industrial buildings carry due diligence items that differ from office or multifamily replacement property, and identifying them early avoids a late surprise inside the exchange period that could otherwise threaten the closing timeline:

  • Clear height, dock door count, and trailer court capacity against the target tenant's operational needs
  • Rail spur access or highway frontage relevant to the specific corridor
  • Environmental history, particularly for sites with prior industrial or energy-service use
  • Power service capacity for manufacturing or cold-storage tenants
  • Existing lease term and tenant credit if the property is being acquired occupied

sections.heading: Timing Industrial Identification Against Corridor Absorption
sections.html:
Well-located industrial buildings along San Antonio's active corridors tend to move quickly once listed, so identification strategy generally involves engaging a corridor-specific broker relationship before the relinquished sale closes rather than starting a search once the 45-day clock is already running. A back-up identification within the same corridor, or a DST interest holding industrial assets, gives the exchange a completion path if the primary building goes under contract with another buyer first.

Value pricing relative to Austin and Dallas keeps San Antonio industrial product competitive for exchange buyers, but that same value proposition draws steady outside capital into the corridors, which is part of why well-priced buildings do not stay on market long.

sections.heading: Environmental and Zoning Considerations for Former Energy-Service Sites
sections.html:
Industrial parcels along the I-10 corridor that previously supported Eagle Ford-related energy service operations - equipment yards, pipe storage, fluid handling - carry environmental review considerations that a standard warehouse acquisition would not, and those reviews can take longer than the 45-day identification window allows if they are not started early. A Phase I environmental assessment, and in some cases a Phase II, should be scoped as soon as a candidate property is identified rather than waiting until the replacement contract is signed.

Zoning and use-permit history also matters for these sites, since a change from prior energy-service use to a new tenant's operations may require a permit review with the city or county before the property can be put back into productive use, which affects both underwriting and the realistic closing timeline.

faqs.q: Which San Antonio corridor generally has the deepest industrial inventory?
faqs.a: The I-35 corridor typically carries the broadest distribution and logistics inventory, given cross-border trade movement and growth extending south from the Austin metro.

faqs.q: Does an identification notice need to specify the industrial use type, or just the address?
faqs.a: The address or legal description satisfies the identification standard on its own; describing the use type is good underwriting practice but not a legal requirement of the identification notice itself.

faqs.q: Are Eagle Ford-adjacent industrial properties riskier for a 1031 replacement?
faqs.a: They carry energy-cycle-specific demand considerations that should be underwritten carefully, but they are not categorically excluded from exchange treatment; diligence should reflect the specific tenant and lease profile.

faqs.q: Can vacant industrial land be identified as replacement property?
faqs.a: Yes, vacant land qualifies as like-kind real property, though an improvement exchange structure is typically needed if the plan is to build out the site within the exchange period.

faqs.q: How much lead time is realistic for sourcing industrial replacement property before the 45-day deadline?
faqs.a: Starting the search before the relinquished property closes is standard practice, since well-located industrial buildings in active San Antonio corridors can go under contract within days of listing.
